title: SMTP credentials should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: SMTP credentials detected in connection string. SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) credentials embedded in connection URLs provide full authentication access to email servers, enabling unauthorized email sending, potential spam distribution, server abuse, and access to email sending quotas. Exposing SMTP credentials creates critical security risks: attackers can send emails impersonating your domain or organization, distribute spam or phishing emails using your infrastructure, exhaust email sending quotas and rate limits, damage your domain and IP reputation leading to blacklisting, access email logs and metadata if permissions allow, modify SMTP server settings in some configurations, intercept or redirect email traffic, and potentially access stored emails depending on server configuration and permissions. SMTP credentials often provide broader access than specialized email service API keys. This is a high-severity security vulnerability requiring immediate remediation. Take immediate action: revoke or reset the exposed SMTP credentials on the mail server immediately, generate new strong passwords for SMTP authentication, update all applications and services with the new credentials, review email server logs for unauthorized access or suspicious sending patterns, check your domain and IP reputation on email blacklist databases, audit what permissions the exposed credentials had, implement IP whitelisting or firewall rules to restrict SMTP access, store SMTP credentials in secure secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault), use environment variables with restricted access for runtime configuration, never commit SMTP credentials to version control or expose in configuration files, implement secret scanning and monitoring in CI/CD pipelines, use application-specific SMTP accounts with minimal necessary permissions, enable TLS/SSL for all SMTP connections (use smtps://), implement rate limiting and monitoring for unusual email sending patterns, rotate SMTP passwords regularly as part of security policies, use email service providers with OAuth or API keys instead of SMTP when possible, document all SMTP accounts in a secure credential inventory, and educate development teams about credential security and proper secret handling practices.
